coredata.dc:title: One-step post-imprint modification achieve dual-function of glycoprotein fluorescent sensor by "Click Chemistry"
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Tao
Wang, Junping
He, Jianli
Deng, Qiliang
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A novel molecular imprinting fluorescent sensor synthesized avoiding complicated design and synthesis of a fluorescent functional monomer, and without the extra cost of additional fluorescence materials (quantum dots, carbon dots and fluorescein), only by a click reaction making the common protein imprinting polymers upgraded to a fluorescent sensing material which can be applied to the fast detection of glycoproteins. In this study, a common protein imprinted polymer (protein-IP) which containing a disulfide bond in the imprinted cavities was first synthesized, then the disulfide linkage was cleaved by reduction, the exposed thiol groups were used for the click reaction to introduce 4-vinyl phenylboronic acid to improve the imprinted recognition for glycoproteins and convert recognition behavior into fluorescent signal simultaneously in one-step site-specific post-imprinting modifications (PIMs). Good linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0.1–10mgmL−1 by fluorescence assay, comparison of the adsorption capacity of the MIPs before and after modification proved that the method has no negative impact on the imprinting effect and exhibit good imprinting effect for glycoproteins of different molecular size and weight.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Post-imprinting modifications
Click Chemistry
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Glycoprotein

Readout of binding behavior by fluorescent measurements
Preparation and characterization of MIPs and NIPs
Adsorption Selectivity assay of normal-MIPs and NIPs
Adsorption selectivity of the normal-MIPs and the post-MIPs
Adsorption kinetics and adsorption isotherm of post-materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of chemical structure of MDTA and MDTA compared to an “upgrade toolbox”.
Graphical abstract of the method of traditional protein molecular imprinting material (MIPs) upgraded to become fluorescent sensing material.
The adsorption capacity of three types of MIP materials: HRP-IPs, OVA-IPs, BSA-IPs.
The adsorption capacity of the normal-MIPs and the post-MIPs for HRP, OVA, conablumin (glycoproteins), BSA (non-glycoprotein).
(a) Changes in fluorescence spectra after addition of HRP (b) Relative fluorescence changes of HRP-MIP and NIP with addition of HRP solution.
Interference effect of other biological substance (a), selectivity of the post-MIP on HRP under influence of non-glycoproteins, BSA (b).

head.ce:title: One-step post-imprint modification achieve dual-function of glycoprotein fluorescent sensor by "Click Chemistry"

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel molecular imprinting fluorescent sensor synthesized avoiding complicated design and synthesis of a fluorescent functional monomer, and without the extra cost of additional fluorescence materials (quantum dots, carbon dots and fluorescein), only by a click reaction making the common protein imprinting polymers upgraded to a fluorescent sensing material which can be applied to the fast detection of glycoproteins. In this study, a common protein imprinted polymer (protein-IP) which containing a disulfide bond in the imprinted cavities was first synthesized, then the disulfide linkage was cleaved by reduction, the exposed thiol groups were used for the click reaction to introduce 4-vinyl phenylboronic acid to improve the imprinted recognition for glycoproteins and convert recognition behavior into fluorescent signal simultaneously in one-step site-specific post-imprinting modifications (PIMs). Good linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0.1–10mgmL−1 by fluorescence assay, comparison of the adsorption capacity of the MIPs before and after modification proved that the method has no negative impact on the imprinting effect and exhibit good imprinting effect for glycoproteins of different molecular size and weight.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomolecular recognition materials are extremely important in proteomics research, diagnosis and therapeutics. Usually, antibodies and enzymes are used as molecular recognition elements for these purposes because of their high affinity and selectivity toward target molecules (Kirsch et al., 2013). Nevertheless, these molecules possess some disadvantages, including high cost, low stability, and difficulty of mass production.

Molecular imprinting is a technique for preparing specialized recognition polymers in the presence of a template molecule (Wulff, 1995). They are cheap and easy to get. Therefore, molecular imprinting is a promising method for preparing artificial receptors with predetermined binding cavities capable of recognizing target molecules, including biomolecules, such as peptides and proteins (Canfarotta et al., 2016; Zayats et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2015). Molecular imprinting technique (MIT) seemed omnipotent in these past studies, and a large number of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been synthesized for small and large molecules recognition and isolation (Chen et al., 2016). MIT has made an indelible contribution towards monitoring environmental contaminants and food hazardous substances, drug delivery, natural product extraction, proteins, peptides and RNA recognition (Li et al., 2015; Wulff, 2013). However, the development of MIT encounter a bottleneck, missing the upgrading of the product (Verheyen et al., 2011; Wulff, 2013). Researchers have attempted to upgrade traditional MIPs by various methods (Whitcombe et al., 2014). The most prominent respects were molecularly imprinted composite materials (Wu et al., 2015; Jia et al., 2013; Madhuri et al., 2014) and biosensor applications (Cieplak et al., 2015; Schirhagl et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013). However, it is difficult to change the functions and stimuli-response in its imprinted cavities by one-pot synthesis.

Post-imprinting modifications (PIMs) become one of the effective ways to solve these problems (Takeuchi et al., 2015a, 2015b; Sunayama et al., 2014), which are in-cavity chemical modifications to introduce additional functions after the construction of specific binding cavities in MIPs. Post-imprinting modifications (PIMs) can provide site-specific modifications within the molecularly imprinted binding cavities after polymerization, enabling MIPs to become more complex functional materials as were the cases of naturally occurring conjugated proteins. Through PIMs to achieve the introduction of new functions and transformation of functional groups, such as improving imprinting effect and transducing the binding events into fluorescence change (Takeuchi et al., 2015a, 2015b; Sunayama et al., 2014). However, these elegant work were often targeted at a particular template, there was less research about applying the PIMs for “common structural template” and “dummy template” in the field of macromolecule. Meanwhile, PIMs often require the design of specific groups and multi-step reactions, which increase the burden and the entry threshold of researchers. MIT has been able to get a great development due to a simple operation. It is significant to develop a common and convenient PIMs method for these problems above.

Glycoproteins, which are a large family of proteins with great biological and clinical importance, have attracted attention in recent years (Lennarz et al., 2012), especially in proteomics study. A number of approaches have been proposed to imprint glycoproteins (Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b; Bi and Liu, 2013). In these elegant work, MIPs with fluorescent properties are promising (Zhang et al., 2014). MIPs were prepared with fluorophores such as quantum dots (Mao et al., 2012), carbon points (Suga et al., 2013). However, the obtained molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors often show high background fluorescence because of the presence of randomly positioned fluorescent functional monomer residues in the polymer matrixes.

To solve these problems. We have developed a new method of making a common protein MIP upgrade to biosensor fluorescent sensing material. The method was one-step site-specific PIM which can achieve different functions by a simple reaction of “Click Chemistry” (Tang et al., 2014). In order to achieve this method, we used a functional monomer bearing an exchangeable moiety in which a disulfide linkage was assembled that could be cleaved by reduction. After the disulfide linkage was cleaved, the exposed thiol groups were used for the click reaction to introduce additional functions. Meanwhile, the monomer has a similar structure with the conventional functional monomer of methacrylate which bearing a polymerizable methacryl group and a carboxylic acid group for interaction with a target protein through a traditional way of hydrogen bonds, ({[2-(2-methacrylamido)-ethyldithio]ethylcarbamoyl}methoxy)acetic acid (MDTA, Fig. 1). The method can be compared to “screwdriver toolbox” in routine maintenance work, the disulfide linkage is like the handle of the screwdriver which can replace different screw heads for different functions, and the functional groups can be compared to the replaceable “screw heads”. Due to the mild “thiol–ene” click reactions. It can make the conversion of functional groups in common MIPs simpler to operate.

In this study, a dual-function of fluorescence property and advanced site-specific recognition biosensor for glycoproteins was achieved by one-step PIM of click reaction within a common protein-IP. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was employed as a template, and HRP-imprinted polymers (HRP-IPs) were prepared by MDTA, and N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBAA) as a cross-linker, in the presence of HRP. Following polymerization, HRP was removed and the disulfide linkages of the MDTA residues were reduced by tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), yielding free thiol residues within the HRP binding cavities. The exposed thiol residues were treated with boronate groups bearing an unsaturated bond by the click reaction to improve the recognition capability of glycoproteins. The one-step site-specific PIM not only introduces additional functional groups which have advanced affinity with glycoprotein (Li et al., 2015) but also endows the MIPs good fluorescence property ( Fig. 2).

N,N′-Diisopropylethylamine, sodium chloroacetate, sodium chloride, sodium sulfate, citric acid, sodium hydrogen carbonate, diethyl ether, methanol, dichloromethane sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate), acetic acid glacial (AcOH), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), acetic acid, triethylamine,n-hexane, ethyl acetate and tris-(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), Methacryloyl chloride, acrylamide, N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBAA), 4-Vinylphenylboronic acid, diglycolic anhydride, ammonium persulfate (APS), 2,2′-azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN), Di-tert-butyldicarbonate, cystamine dihydrochloride, tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), N,N,N′,N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) obtained from Sangon BioTech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), ovalbumin (OVA), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), conalbumin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of designed functional monomer, MDTA
ce:section.ce:para: The new functional monomer MDTA was prepared by a modified method reported by Hirobumi Sunayama et al. (Sunayama et al., 2014). In the process of preparation of N-tert-butoxycarbonyl cystamine, boc anhydride (2.18g, 10mmol) and triethylamine (4.2mL, 3 equiv) were added to 15mL of methanol, and the as-prepared solution was dripped slowly into a methanol solution of cystamine dihydrochloride (2.25g, 10mmol) within 20min in an ice bath. N-tert-butoxycarbonyl cystamine was extracted with chloroform instead of ethyl acetate in the last step. All modifications in order to improve the yield of N-tert-butoxycarbonyl cystamine. Details were shown in Supporting information.

30mg template and 332mg MDTA were firstly dissolved in the 20mL of 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0), and then incubated for 30min at 4°C. After nitrogen bubbling for 5min to remove the oxygen, 308mg MBAA, 200µl of 10% APS (w/v) and 100µl of 5% TEMED (v/v) were added to the well mixed solution. The polymerization reaction was carried out at 25°C for 24h. After the reaction, the resultant MIPs were freeze-dried in a Virtis freeze drier (Christ Company, Germany) for two days to completely remove water and then pressed through a 70-mesh sieve to produce granules for further studies. Non-imprinted polymers were prepared in the same way without adding template. The template was removed from the granulated gel particles by successive washing with 100mL RO-water, 10% SDS/AcOH (100mL, 1:9, v/v) and 200mL RO-water. The protein concentration in the wash fractions was determined by UV detection.

For cleavage of the disulfde linkage, the obtained HRP-IPs were dispersed in 50mM TCEP solution and the reaction was mechanically stirred with nitrogen protection at room temperature for 24h. The products were washed with water three times, then dried overnight.

The disulfide-cleaved HRP-IPs, 295.94mg 4-vinyl phenylboronic acid and 10mg AIBN were dispersed in MeOH/H2O (v/v, 1/1, 20mL) and the materials were mechanically stirred with nitrogen protection at 60°C for 24h to introduce boronate groups in the HRP-IPs cavities.

FT-IR spectras (4000-400cm−1) in KBr were recorded using a Vector 22 spectrometer (Bruker, brukeroptics. equips. cn/). The X-ray photoelectron spectra was obtained on a Shimadzu (Japan) Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with Mg Kα (15kV, 400W) at a takeoff angle of 45° irradiation as X-ray source were conducted to study the elemental composition. Fluorescence (FL) measurements were carried out on a Thermo Scientific Lumina FL spectrometer (America) equipped with a 1×1cm quartz cell. UV–vis spectra were measured on a Thermo Scientific Evolution 300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (America). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing the surface morphologies of the MIP and NIP were obtained using a Hitachi SU1510 electron microscope (Japan). The results of XPS, FT-IR spectras and SEM were shown in Supporting information.

The capacity of normal MIPs and NIPs, MIPs and NIPs after Post-imprinting modifications (post-MIPs, post-NIPs) toward glycoproteins and non-glycoproteins was carried out by incubating materials (5mg) in a solution (5mL) of protein samples with different concentrations (0.1mgmL−1 to 1.0mgmL−1) in 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0) at room temperature overnight. Then the materials were centrifuged and the supernatant was determined by using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer. The experimental data are presented as the adsorption capacity per unit mass (mg) of the materials, and the adsorption capacity (Q) is calculated using the equation below:
Q =
(
C
0
−
C
t
)
Vm
− 1
× 10
3
( mg
g
− 1
)

Here C0 (mgmL−1) is the initial concentration of protein solution; Ct (mgmL−1) is the equilibrium concentration of the protein; V (mL) is the volume of the protein solution; m (mg) is the mass of MIPs. Contrast adsorption experiments were also performed with OVB, conalbumin and BSA solutions under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Readout of binding behavior by fluorescent measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The post-HRP-IPs were placed in 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0), and fluorescence was measured (λex: 365nm/λEm: 403nm, 25°C. 1cm cell) by a Hitachi F-2500 spectro fuorometer (Hitachi, Japan) with various concentrations of HRP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of MIPs and NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal molar ratio of traditional synthetic molecular imprinting was 1:4:20 (template/monomer/crosslinker) for low molecular weight compounds. However, for large template molecules, high crosslink densities seriously hinder mass transfer of the template, leading to slow template removal and rebinding kinetics or, in the worst case, permanent entrapment of the template in the polymer network due to physical immobilization. We optimize the synthesis ratio and obtain the optimal molar ratio was 1:2 (monomer / crosslinker). Details were shown in Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption Selectivity assay of normal-MIPs and NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: To choose the best MIPs materials for PIMs, three proteins were used as templates for synthesis of normal-MIPs. HRP and ovalbumin (OVA) are the glycoproteins which has the similar molecular weight and structures, bovine serum albumin (BSA) is the non-glycoprotein. The adsorption capacity was investigated by Langmuir's adsorption isotherm through a UV-detector. The results in Fig. 3 showed the HRP-IPs have the best imprint effect compared with the OVA-IPs and BSA-IPs.

The HRP-IPs showed the best affinity to template, so we chose the HRP-IPs for PIMs. The process as follows: the materials were first reduced by TCEP yielding free thiol residues within the binding cavities and the exposed thiol was utilized to introduce multi-function groups by one-step click reaction. Using a variety of functional monomers bearing an unsaturated bond can improve the selectivity for the templates or endow the normal-MIPs additional functions, such as fluorescent monomers, enzyme groups and other functional groups (Hoyle et al., 2010; Murase et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption selectivity of the normal-MIPs and the post-MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the result of the adsorption selectivity of the normal-MIPs and the post-MIPs. The proteins of HRP, OVA, conalbumin were used as glycoproteins and BSA as the non-glycoprotein.

We first compared the adsorption capacity of post-MIPs for glycoproteins and non-glycoproteins. The results in Fig. 4 showed that the adsorption capacity for three types of glycoproteins was significantly increased, but no obvious increase occurs for the non-glycoprotein (BSA) after post modification. The results indicated the introduction of additional monomer of 4-vinyl phenylboronic acid has influenced specific recognition in the post-MIPs by boronate groups due to a covalent bond formed instead of the weak hydrogen bonds between HRP and materials.

Then, we focused on the reason why the adsorption capacity of the three glycoproteins increased in various degrees. The results in Fig. 4 showed that improvement of adsorption capacity varied from different types of glycoproteins, among them the OVA increased the maximum and the conalbumin increased the minimum. The reason may be the HRP which as the template occupied a sufficient number of imprinting sites in normal-MIPs, the adsorption saturation at a relatively high level due to the limit of unoccupied specific recognition sites of normal-MIPs, the OVA which has similar molecular weight and size to the template of HRP can be captured more amount to enter the post-MIPs networks due to a large number of unoccupied imprinting sites and a low adsorption saturation in normal-MIPs. However, for the conalbumin too lager molecular weight and molecular size blocked access to the post-MIPs cavities leading to only a small amount conalbumins were captured in the periphery of the post-MIPs networks.

The result above indicated that PIMs have no negative effects on the ability of imprint recognition and improve the recognition capability by introducing specific functional groups in the MIPs recognition cavity. The adsorption capacity in various degrees after PIMs which indicated the materials could be utilized to distinguish the glycoproteins of different molecular size and weight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption kinetics and adsorption isotherm of post-materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S11 (a) showed the equilibrium concentration dependence of the adsorbed amount of HRP onto the post-MIP material. The adsorption capacity increased with increasing concentration of HRP and a saturation capacity was obtained at a HRP concentration of 0.6mgmL−1, which represented saturation of the reaction sites of the material. The saturated adsorption capacity was evaluated to be 223.63mgg −1.

Adsorption kinetics experiment was carried out for the post-MIPs materials by using 0.6mgmL−1 HRP solution. The adsorption curve was shown in Fig. S11 (b). It can be seen that the material had a rapid increase in adsorption with the time extension and the adsorption capacity reaching up to maximum of 8h.

The ideas of developing fluorescence property of the materials came from an interesting phenomenon in the process of MDTA synthesis. The new functional monomer showed weak fluorescence properties under the UV-light irradiation at 365nm (Fig. S1), so we assumed that we can use the fluorescence property of functional monomer for the detection of glycoprotein. As a result of the weak fluorescence intensity, the normal-MIPs can't be used for fluorescence detection. We have to improve the fluorescence properties of the functional monomer to meet the application of fluorescence detection. In general, there are two ways. One is introducing additional fluorophores directly (Zhou et al., 2014) and the other way is improving the fluorescence property of the new functional monomer by chemical synthesis (Breul et al., 2013). But both of them have drawbacks that the introduction of other fluorophores may be too costly and chemical modification requires synthesis design and purification. Due to the group-exchangeable functional monomer, we assumed we can use one-step site-specific PIM to avoid these disadvantages. We tried to introduce multi-function groups bearing fluorophores and boronate groups by click reaction.

In this study we used 4-vinyl phenylboronic acid to introduce boronate groups through the click reaction which can not only form a five-membered ring with saccharides in the recognition cavity of MIPs but also contain conjugated large π bond to improve the fluorescence property of MIPs as a fluorescence reporter (Ivanov et al., 2006; Beppu et al., 2015).

To develop applications of these MIPs materials, we have developed post-MIP material as a fluorescent sensing material to detect glycoprotein. A new method combining specific recognition property of MIP and fluorescent property was established for detection of glycoproteins. We first tested the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of the materials, the materials has a maximum excitation wavelength at 365nm and a maximum emission wavelength at 430nm, while the target glycoproteins HRP has no effect in the range of emission. The relative change in fluorescent intensity (FL) decreased with the addition of HRP ( Fig. 5a). On this basis, we have made a comparison of selectivity capacity between post-MIP and post-NIP materials under the optimal detection conditions of 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0) at 25°C, the value of post-MIPs could be saturated at 10mgmL−1, indicating that HRP can be bound to the limited number of binding sites in the imprinted cavities, the binding sites were labelled by fluorophore which were introduced through PIMs. (Fig. 5b).

The mechanism of the decrease in FL intensity may be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The UV absorption wavelength of HRP (403nm) is close to the emission wavelength of the post-MIPs (430nm), and the fluorescence was quenched mainly due to the specific interaction between the post-MIPs and the template HRP when the template protein HRP close to the imprinted sites in recognition process (Fig. S14). The FL quenching in this system followed the Stern-Volmer equation.
F
0
/ F = 1 + K
sv
[ Q ]

F0 and F were the FL intensity of the materials in the absence and presence of HRP, respectively, Ksv was the Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] was the quencher concentration. This equation was used to quantify the different quenching constants in this research, and the ratio of the Ksv values of the post-MIP and post-NIP was defined as the imprinting factor to evaluate the selectivity of the materials. Good linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0.1–10mgmL−1 and the imprinting factor for post-MIPs of HRP was 1.94.

To further validate the application of the material in real samples, we extracted HRP from horseradish with the synthetic post-MIPs. The assay procedure was shown in Supporting information (Fig. S13).

In order to further investigate the performance of the post-MIPs, OVA, CON, BSA and Cyt c were chosen as competitive protein to evaluate the selectivity of post-MIPs. The selectivity experiment was performed for OVA, CON, BSA, Cyt c and other biological substance with a same concentration of 10mgmL−1. We chose HRP concentration of 10mgmL−1 due to the fluorescence intensity of post-MIPs could be saturated at 10mgmL−1. It was clearly seen that the changes in fluorescence intensity of post-MIP to HRP were much more obvious than that to glycoproteins of OVA and CON. However, the changes in fluorescence intensity of post-MIPs were not significantly different for BSA, Cyt c and other biological substance. The imprinted sites and cavies with complementary in size, shape and functional group to the template protein were formed in synthesis process. As the competitive protein, although OVA is similar to HRP in the molecular volume and structures, the recognition sites were not complementary to the competitive protein. Thus, the fluorescence intensity was not obviously changed by the competitive protein ( Fig. 6a).

To further demonstrate the influence of non-glycoproteins on the selectivity of post-MIPs for glycoproteins, binary competitive adsorption experiment was performed. The binary adsorption experiment was carried out by preparing a series of protein solution of HRP–BSA and fixing the concentration of HRP (10mgmL−1) and increasing the concentration of BSA. It can be seen from Fig. 6b that the intensity of fluorescence was not significantly affected as the ratio of CHRP/CBSA increased, indicating non-glycoproteins have no influence on the recognition of post-MIPs for glycoproteins.

A common protein imprinting polymers upgrades to a fluorescent sensing material owning the special recognition property of molecular imprinting technique and fluorescence property to detect glycoproteins through a convenient method of one-step site-specific PIMs. The method has no negative effects on the force of imprint recognition and improves the recognition capability simultaneously. The one-step site-specific PIMs also endow the materials fluorescence property to be able to transduce the binding events into fluorescence changes making the detection of glycoproteins more precise and easier to operate. What's more, due to functional group-exchangeable monomer, a variety of functional monomers can be introduced by mild “thiol–ene” click reactions and disulfide exchange reaction, bringing the traditional MIPs a more wide application prospect.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully appreciate the financial support by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFD0401202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2017.01.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Biochemistry of Glycoproteins and Proteoglycans
Post-imprinting and In-Cavity Functionalization. Molecularly Imprinted Polymers in Biotechnology
